title: Persistent Homology Captures the Generalization of Neural Networks Without A Validation Set

ABSTRACT

The training of neural networks is usually monitored with a validation (holdout) set to estimate the generalization of the model. This is done instead of measuring intrinsic properties of the model to determine whether it is learning appropriately. In this work, we suggest studying the training of neural networks with Algebraic Topology, specifically Persistent Homology (PH). Using simplicial complex representations of neural networks, we study the PH diagram distance evolution on the neural network learning process with different architectures and several datasets. Results show that the PH diagram distance between consecutive neural network states correlates with the validation accuracy, implying that the generalization error of a neural network could be intrinsically estimated without any holdout set.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Generalization is what makes a machine learning model useful; the uncertainty of its behaviour with unseen data is what makes it potentially dangerous. Thus, understanding the generalization error of a model can be considered one of the holy grails of the entire machine learning field.

Machine learning practitioners typically monitor some metrics of the model to estimate its generalization error and stop the training even before the numerical convergence to prevent the overfitting of the model. Usually, the error measure or the metric relevant to the task is computed for a holdout set, the validation set. Since these data have not been directly used for updating the parameters, it is assumed that the performance of the model on the validation set can be used as a proxy of the generalization error, provided it is representative of the data that will be used in inference. One can, though, potentially overfit to this holdout set if is repeatedly used for guiding a hyperparameter search.

Instead of relying on an external set, though, the question of whether it could be possible to estimate the generalization error with some intrinsic property of the model is highly relevant, and it has been

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

barely explored in the literature. On the other hand, Algebraic Topology has recently been gaining momentum as a mathematical tool for studying graphs, machine learning algorithms, and data.

In this work, we have the goal of, once having characterized neural networks as weighted, acyclic graphs, represented as Algebraic Topology objects (following previous works), computing distances between consecutive neural network states. More specifically, we can calculate the Persistent Homology (PH) diagram distances between a give state (i.e., when having a specific weights during the training process) and the next one (i.e., after having updated the weights in a training step), as depicted in Figure 1. We observe that during the training procedure of neural networks we can measure this distance in each learning step, and show that there exists a high correlation with the corresponding validation accuracy of the model. We do so in a diverse set of deep learning benchmarks and model hyperparameters. This shines light on the question of whether the generalization error could be estimated from intrinsic properties of the model, and opens the path towards a better theoretical understanding of the dynamics of the training of neural networks.

In summary, our contributions are as follows:

- Based on principles of Algebraic Topology, we propose measuring the distances (Silhouette and Heat) between the PH persistence diagrams obtained from a given state of a neural network during the training procedure and the one in the immediately previous weights update.

- We empirically show that the evolution of these measures during training correlate with the accuracy in the validation set. We do so in diverse benchmarks (MNIST, CIFAR10, CIFAR100, Reuters text classification), and models (MLPs in MNIST and Reuters, MLPs and CNNs in CIFAR100 and CIFAR100).

- We thus provide empirical proof of the fact that valuable information related to the learning process of neural networks can be obtained from PH distances between persistence diagrams (we will call this process _homological convergence_ ). In particular, we show that homological convergence is related to learning process and the generalization properties of neural networks.
- In practice, we provide a new tool for monitoring the training of neural networks, and open the path to estimating their generalization error without a validation set.

The remainder of this article is as follows. In Section 2 we describe the theoretical background of our proposal in terms of Algebraic Topology, while in Section 3 we go through the related work. Then, in Section 4 we formalize our method. Finally, in sections 6 and 7 we present and discuss our empirical results, respectively.

## 2 BACKGROUND

In this section we introduce the mathematical foundations of this paper. A detailed mathematical description is included in the Supplementary Material.

A simplicial complex is a set composed of points, line segments, triangles, and their n-dimensional counterparts, named simplex ( _K_ ). In particular, a simplicial complex must comply with two properties: 1. Every face of a simplex is also in the simplicial complex (of lower dimension). 2. The non-empty intersection of any two simplices contained on a simplicial complex is a face of both. 0,1,2,3-simplex and non simplex examples are shown in Figure 2.

corresponds to a ( _i −_ 1)-simplex. This type of construction is usually called a complex clique on the graph G, and is denoted by _Cl_ ( _G_ ). Figure 3 shows a graph clique complex Cl(G) example.
The boundary function is defined as a map, from an _i_ -simplex to an ( _i −_ 1)-simplex, as the sum of its ( _i −_ 1)-dimensional faces. A boundary function sample is shown in Figure 4.

In algebraic topology, a _k_ -chain is a combination of _k_ -simplices (sometimes symbolized as a linear combination of simplices that compose the chain). The boundary of a _k_ -chain is a ( _k −_ 1)chain. It is the linear and signed combination of chain element boundary simplices. The space of _i_ -chains is denoted by _Ci_ ( _K_ ).

Figure 3: Graph clique complex Cl(G) example.

There are two special cases of chains that will be useful to define homology group:

- Closed chain or _i_ -cycle: _i_ -chain with empty boundary. An _i_ -chain _c_ is an _i_ -cycle if and only if _∂ic_ = 0, i.e. _c ∈ ker_ ( _∂i_ ). This subspace of _Ci_ ( _K_ ) is denoted as _Zi_ ( _K_ ).

- Exact chain or _i_ -boundary: An _i_ -chain _c_ is an _i_ -boundary if there exists an ( _i_ + 1)-chain _d_ such that _c_ = _∂i_ +1( _d_ ), i.e. _c ∈ im_ ( _∂ i_ + 1). This subspace of _Ci_ ( _K_ ), the set of all such i-boundaries forms, is denoted by _Bi_ ( _K_ ).

Now, if we consider _i_ -cycles not bounding an ( _i_ + 1)-simplicial complex, this is the definition of an _i_ -th homology group of the simplicial complex _K_ . The precise definition is the quotient group of _Bi_ ( _K_ ) module _Zi_ ( _K_ ) (i.e. _Bi_ ( _K_ ) _/Zi_ ( _K_ ), see Supplementary Material). The number of non equivalent _i_ -cycles (Figure 5) is the dimension of the homology group _Hi_ ( _K_ ), also named Betti numbers.

Figure 5: The two blue dashed cycles are homologically equivalent, the pink isn’t.

We can create a nested family of simplicial complexes, _Kε_ , where at each step _t_ , _Kεt_ is embedded in the simplicial complex _Kεt_ +1 . We call this set a simplicial complex filtration.

For each filtration simplicial complex, we can calculate the homology groups. Then, we can look at the birth, that is, when a homology class appears, and death, the time when the homology class disappears. The PH treats the birth and the death of these homological features in _Kε_ for different _ε_ values. The lifespan of each homological feature can be represented as an interval ( _birth, death_ ), of the homological class. Given a filtration, this collection of intervals is named a Persistence Diagram (PD) Carlsson (2009).

It is possible to compare two PDs using specific

distances (Wasserstein and Bottleneck). To efficiently perform this operation, due to the size of these diagrams, it is sometimes necessary to simplify them by means of a discretization process (such as Weighted Silhouette and Heat vectorizations).

## 3 RELATED WORK

**Algebraic Topology and Machine Learning** The use of Algebraic Topology in the fields of data science and machine learning has been gaining momentum in recent years (see Carlsson (2009)). Specifically in the case of neural networks, some works have applied topology for improving the training procedure of the models Hofer et al. (2020); Clough et al. (2020), or pruning the model afterwards Watanabe & Yamana (2020b). Other works have focused on analyzing the capacity of neural networks Guss & Salakhutdinov (2018a); Rieck et al. (2019b); Konuk & Smith (2019) or the complexity of input data Konuk & Smith (2019). Furthermore, recent works have provided topological analysis of the decision boundaries of classifiers based on PH and Betti numbers Ramamurthy et al. (2019); Naitzat et al. (2020).

**Graph and topological representations of neural networks** Gebhart et al. (2019) suggest a method for computing the PH over the graphical activation neural networks, while Watanabe & Yamana (2020a) propose representing neural networks via simplicial complexes based on Taylor decomposition, from which one can compute the PH. Chowdhury et al. (2019) show that directed homology can be used to represent MLPs. Anonymous (2021) concurrently show neural networks, when represented as directed, acyclic graphs, can be associated to an Algebraic Topology object, specifically to a directed flag complex. By computing the PH diagram, one can effectively characterize neural networks, and even compute distances between two given neural networks, which can be used to measure their similarity. This is unlike other works Corneanu et al. (2019); Guss & Salakhutdinov (2018b) approximating neural networks representations with regard to the input space. Relevant to our work, in Rieck et al. (2019b) authors propose a complexity measure of neural networks based on persistent homology. However, we will see that their representation does not fulfill our requirements in Section 4.

**Estimating the generalization and studying the learning process** We are, though, specifically interested in the use of PH for analyzing the learning process, especially with the goal of estimating generalization. In this regard, the literature is perhaps more limited. Jiang et al. (2019) work on understanding what drives generalization in deep networks from a Bayesian of view. Neyshabur et al. (2017) study the generalization gap prediction from the training data and network parameters using a margin distribution, which are the distances of training points to the decision boundary. In Li et al. (2020), authors propose an alternative to cross-validation for model selection based on training once on the whole train set, without any data split, deriving a validation set with data augmentation.

Corneanu et al. (2020) try to estimate the performance gap between training and testing using PH measures. They claim. However, one can observe some caveats. The first one is that their regression fitted to predict the test error has a considerably high error, making it not usable in practice. The second caveat is that for fitting the regression one needs at least part of the sequestered testing set.

In this work, motivated by the interest of having a better understanding of whether it would be possible to estimate the generalization of neural networks without a holdout set, we suggest using the topological characterization and distances concurrently proposed in Anonymous (2021) but, crucially, measured between consecutive weight updates. We will show that the evolution of this distance is similar to the one of the validation accuracy. Unlike Li et al. (2020), we do not use any data at all. Unlike Corneanu et al. (2020), we do not build a statistical or machine learning model (linear regression) for predicting the testing error. Instead, we propose a new measure, and we empirically show that it highly correlates with the validation accuracy. Note that in this work we do not work with any input data and activations, but with the parameters of the neural network themselves. The code and outputs are fully available in the Supplementary Material under a MIT License.

## 4 APPROACH

**Representation** For representing neural networks as graphs, we follow the approach proposed concurrently in Anonymous (2021). We associate to the neural network, at each learning state (defined by its weights), a weighted directed graph that is analyzed as an abstract simplicial complex. It is important to note that abstract simplicial complex are used in opposition to geometric simplicial complex (e.g. Vietoris-Rips complex).

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

For every training state, neural network connections are considered as directed and weighted edges between neurons, represented by graph nodes. Biases are considered as new edges that join to isolate vertices. In this representation, activation functions are lost. Bias information could also have been ignored because, as we will see, it is not very informative in terms of homology, but we decided to preserve it.

Negative edge weights are represented with reverse edges with the same weight absolute value. We discard the use of the absolute value of weights as neural networks are not invariant to weight sign transformations. This representation is consistent with the fact that every neuron can be replaced by a neuron from which two edges with opposite weights emerge and converge again on another neuron with opposite weights. From an homological point of view, this would be represented as a closed cycle. Weights are normalized following the Equation 1. _ζ_ is an smoothing parameter that we set to 1e-6. This smoothing parameter is necessary as we want to avoid normalized weights of edges to be 0 (in our representation 0 implies a lack of connection):

Unlike Rieck et al. (2019b) we do not represent the neural network as a multipartite graph with a persistent homology filtration that contains at most 1-simplices (edges), which only capturrd zero-dimensional topological information, i.e. connectivity information. We do not believe the strong assumption that the NNs encode the learned information layer pairwise exclusively since there are trivial global transformations of a NN that are not captured by analyzing pairs of layers, more specifically 1. Superfluous cycle insertions: for instance, add two neurons and connect their input to a single neuron in a lower layer and their two outputs to a single output neuron in an upper layer with opposite weights; 2. Identity layer insertions: insert an intermediate identity layer with neurons and trivially connect to the next layer; and 3. Non-planar neural networks analysis: the analysis of neural networks that use multiple connections between non-consecutive layers require higher order topological analysis.

**Algebraic Topology object** For each weighted directed graph associated with the state of a neural network, we link a directed flag complex to it. The topological properties of this directed flag complex are studied using homology groups _Hn_ . We calculate the homology groups up to degree 3 ( _H_ 0- _H_ 3).

For each state, we use a family of simplicial complexes, _Kε_ , for a range of values of _ε ∈_ R. The simplicial complex at step _εt_ is embedded in the complex at _εt_ +1, for _εt ≤ εt_ +1, i.e. _Kε ⊆ Kεt_ +1. _ε_ is used as a filter that establish the minimum weight of the graph representation edges included on the simplicial complex. This collection of contained simplicial complex (associated to a directed weighted graph), called filtration, _Kεmin ⊆ ... ⊆ Kεt ⊆ Kεt_ +1 _⊆ ... ⊆ Kεmax_ , where _t ∈_ [0 _,_ 1] and _εmin_ = 0, _εmax_ = 1 (remember that edge weights are normalized).

The sequence of homology groups is calculated by varying the _ε_ parameter to obtain the persistence homology diagram. In our case, persistent homology calculations are performed on Z2. In other words, once the corresponding filter has been applied to the weight of the edges, all connected edges are considered equally.

**Distances between persistence diagrams of consecutive states** In this paper, we are interested in comparing PDs between different simplicial complex associated to each training state of the neural network. There are two distances traditionally used to compare PDs, Bottleneck distance (the length of the longest edge) and Wasserstein distance (using the sum of all edges lengths, instead of the maximum). Their stability with respect to perturbations on PDs has been object of different studies Chazal et al. (2012); Cohen-Steiner et al. (2005). As shown in comparative studies such as in **?** , different distances and different ways of vectorizing persistence diagrams have results with different levels of stability and quality.

In order to make computations feasible and obviate noisy intervals, we filter the PDs by limiting the minimum PD interval size. We do so by setting a minimum threshold _η_ = 0 _._ 01. Intervals with a lifespan under this value are not considered (spurious homological features). Additionally, for computing distances, we need to remove infinity values. As we are only interested in the deaths until the maximum weight value, we replace all the infinity values by 1 _._ 0.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

In our case, neural networks have millions of persistence intervals per PD, while Wasserstein distance calculations are computationally hard for large PDs. To make it computationally feasible, we will use a vectorized version of PDs, also called PD discretization. This vectorized version summaries have been proposed and used on recent literature Adams et al. (2017); Berry et al. (2020); Bubenik (2015); Lawson et al. (2019); Rieck et al. (2019a). For persistence diagram distance calculation, we use weighted Silhouette and Heat vectorizations, using the Giotto-TDA library Tauzin et al. (2020).

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

**Data** We validate our method in several heterogeneous (vision, natural language), well-known datasets, namely 1. MNIST LeCun & Cortes (2010), 2. CIFAR-10, 3. CIFAR-100 Krizhevsky (2009), and 4. the Reuters dataset Thoma (2017) (multi-class and multi-label document classification dataset).

**Models** We experiment with two neural architectures,1. MLPs and 2. CNNs. In the latter case, we use the convolutional layers as a pre-trained model with frozen weights, and we learn an MLP on top of it. The reason we do so is that our method is based in a representation that, at least in the basic form, does not allow capturing information from convolutional layers. Thus, we need a single (same weights) feature extractor, to abstract away distances related to the CNN layers and focus on the MLP.

**Conducted experiments** We define the _base_ MLP architecture as {Input, Linear(512), Dropout(0.2), Linear(512), Dropout(0.2), Output}. In the case of CNNs, the pre-trained model is defined as 3 convolutional blocks with kernel size 3 (starting with 32 channels), interleaved with max pooling (its linear layers are thrown away after the pre-training). On top of the pre-trained CNN, we also define the same base MLP architecture.

Then, for each dataset and model (MLP and CNN), we experiment with varying (while keeping the rest fixed to the base architecture) 1. Layer size (number of units per layer): 4, 16, 32, 128, 256; 2. Number of labels (the other classes are removed): 2, 4, 6, 8, 10; 3. Learning rate: 1e-e05, 0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1; 4. Dropout: 0.0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.5, 0.8; and 5. Input order: 5 random reordering of the input samples. As a control experiment, for each analyzed problem we run the same configuration with 5 different input orders. If the measured distances are, indeed, related with the learning process of neural networks, these variations should not have any noticeable effect. We run each configuration 5 times with different random seeds (and, thus, weight initializations[1] ) to see if the results are consistent across runs. All models are trained with the RMSProp optimizer with a batch size of 256.

**Distances and validation accuracy computation** Note that homological distances are obtained at the end of each batch, while validation metrics are only computed on each epoch. We consider the validation curve to be our baseline, and therefore we compare our method to it. The methodology is summarized as follows:
1. In each training step (i.e., batch) we extract the weights from the MLP current state and use them to build an abstract simplicial complex from the associated weighted directed graph.

2. We calculate the homological persistence diagram of the simplicial complex.

3. We then calculate the distance between consecutive persistence diagrams (we will call this sequence _homological convergence_ ). We use two different distances, Heat and Silhouette.
4. We compare the homological convergence with the evolution of the validation results on neural network learning process.

All experiments were executed in a machine with 2 NVIDIA V100 of 32GB, 2 Intel(R) Xeon(R) Platinum 8176 CPU @ 2.10GHz, and of 1.5TB RAM, for a total of around 7 days. We note that our method is considerably demanding in terms of both compute and memory.

## 6 RESULTS

In this section, we highlight the main results, omitting Silhouette (since the results were clearer with Heat). See the Supplementary Material for all the plots and correlations, including Silhouette.

Figure 6: Heat distance and validation accuracy curves on MNIST with layer size. Normalized.

For each experiment (e.g., layer size in MNIST), we plot both the evolution of the PH diagram distance and the validation score (accuracy). The plotted values are the corresponding means of the 5 repetitions with different seeds. In addition, we compute the Pearson correlation for these values. Plots show on the x-axis each training step (for each batch) of the evolution in the training state of the neural network. On the y-axis, two scales are shown that apply to the distance curves between accumulated persistence diagrams (solid lines), scale on the right side, and the neural network validation (dotted lines), numerical scale on the left side. For each sub-experiment (for example, different values of layer size) a different color was used.

The general result is that the evolution of the homological convergence of the MLPs seems to be very similar to the one of the validation score. This is generally consistent across experiments (see the Supplementary Material). Table 1 shows the mean (and standard deviations) of the Pearson correlations for all datasets. All means are above 0.8, implying that there is strong correlation. Intuitively, this is also observed in the plots, although once the distances are normalized it is not as clear to visualize. Interestingly, we find that the very few exceptions in which the correlation is low corresponds to extreme values (very small number of neurons per layer, very high learning rate, very high dropout), in which the neural network doesn’t end up learning properly.

In CNNs, the correlations are lower (but still usually above 0.8 in experiments such as the one of increasing the number of layers). Recall that we froze a convolutional feature extractor since our method only supports MLPs. We believe these lower correlations occur because an important part of the learning process happened in the convolutional layers (in the pre-training), not captured.

Another finding is that the method obtains consistent results across runs, meaning that it is capturing information related to important properties of the networks themselves instead of random artifacts. When varying the studied hyperparameters, we observe that the curves for each configuration are indeed, different. Remarkably, in the control experiments, this is not the case; results show that the homological convergence during the learning of the same problem with the same model but with different input order is very similar. The alteration of the order of the input doesn’t have any effect in the homological convergence. The results of two of these experiments are shown in Figure 7.

In addition, we observe that when the neural network learns the given problem, homological convergence occurs. For example, when the layer size is modified, the capacity of the neural network to learn the problem changes (Figure 6). When it can’t learn the problem, because the network does not have sufficient capacity (the layer size is too small, 4 units), the homology does not seem to converge.

Regarding the learning rate, the results are coherent with the intuition that it is a fundamental parameter that controls how much to change the model in response to the estimated error during the learning process. A too small learning rate may result in a long training process that could be stalled, while a too large value may fall in a fast suboptimal solution or an unstable training process. Using homological convergence we find similar behaviour, as can be seen in Figure 9.

Finally, we note that even if the two convergences (validation and homological convergence) are correlated, they are not the same process. This is especially visible in the learning rate experiments. For instance, in Figure 9, homological convergence is reached before the stabilization of the validation accuracy. Presumably, they are not capturing the exact same information; we believe that the difference is due to the fact that the validation accuracy depends on the specifics of the data sampled in the validation subset, while the homological convergence is independent of the validation data.

## 7 DISCUSSION

We posed the question whether homological convergence (in terms of distances between PH diagrams in consecutive neural network states) is related to the learning process of neural networks. We have seen that, indeed, it is the case, with strong empirical results backing our claim. This finding has a remarkable implication. If homological convergence evolution mirrors the validation accuracy curve, one could ignore the validation set to monitor the training. This opens the path towards estimating the generalization of neural networks without the need of any holdout set. Researchers have wondered for a long time whether generalization could be predicted from intrinsic properties of the model or training data alone, and in fact other works have claimed to do so. Although we do not provide any predictive model, we show that our proposed measures strongly correlate with validation accuracy. We do so by not using any data at all; we just look at the neural network itself. Our contribution aims pushing towards a better understanding of the learning process of neural networks, not targeting any specific direct application. However, we note that it can be effectively used for monitoring the training of neural networks in terms of convergence expected generalization, as we have extensively

shown in the experiments. Apart from when there is no access to a validation set, this is relevant because depending on a validation set has the risk of overfitting to it. Having an intrinsic measure should be more robust to random noise in a specific data sample.

The main limitation of our method is its computational scalability. This lack of scalability has prevented us from validating our method on bigger models and datasets. However, we note that our approach computes the _exact_ PD distances, that is, we do not simplify the graph representation of the neural networks (we keep every single neuron and connections) and we do not approximate any computation. This leaves room for finding efficient approximations, opening a new research line.

Finally, we note that instead of computing correlations, serving as a basic quantitative study, it would be interesting to perform a time-series analysis to gain more insights on how the two curves vary together. Moreover, it would have been interesting to investigate how to build a predictive model of the validation accuracy from the PH distances, but it is was of the scope of this work.

## 8 CONCLUSIONS & FUTURE WORK

In this work, we have provided an empirical proof of the fact that homological convergence is related to the learning process and generalization properties of neural networks. Furthermore, we have shown that it can be used to monitor the training of a neural network (and potentially estimating its generalization) without a validation set. As future work, we suggest generalizing our representation to other neural architectures and scaling up the experiments to larger models and datasets, for which finding efficient approximations of our method will be crucial.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes an innovative application of persistent homology to track neural network generalization without relying on validation sets, aligning with growing interest in intrinsic diagnostics for deep learning. The core idea—using topological summaries of network states as proxies for generalization—is conceptually compelling and sits within an emerging line of work connecting TDA to model behavior. The visual framing in Figure 1 suggests a clear narrative, and the use of diagram distances is methodologically sound in principle.

Overview issues:

- Lack of control for optimization dynamics and initialization effects: The paper claims that PH diagram distance evolution correlates with validation accuracy, yet it does not account for confounding factors such as optimization path, learning rate schedules, or random initialization. These factors are known to influence both training trajectories and generalization behavior. Without multiple random seeds or controlled ablation studies (e.g., same architecture with different initializations showing divergent PH trends despite similar validation curves), the observed correlation may reflect stochastic training artifacts rather than intrinsic generalization signals. This weakens the claim of universality. The authors should include variance plots across seeds and test whether PH distances remain predictive under different optimization regimes.

- Unclear construction of simplicial complexes from network states: The method relies on representing neural networks as simplicial complexes, but the paper does not specify how these representations are built—such as whether nodes correspond to neurons, layers, or weight matrices, or how filtrations are defined (e.g., via activation correlations, weight magnitudes, or gradient flows). This omission makes the approach irreproducible and raises concerns about representational validity. Given that different constructions (e.g., Vietoris-Rips vs. clique complexes) can yield vastly different persistence diagrams, the choice must be justified and evaluated for stability. A concrete algorithmic description and sensitivity analysis are required.

- Overstated universality of PH-distance correlation without cross-architecture validation: The claim that PH diagram distance 'captures generalization' implies broad applicability, but the experiments appear limited to specific architectures and datasets without evidence of transfer across model families (e.g., CNNs vs. Transformers) or data distributions. Prior work (e.g., Birdal et al. 2021, Sanchez et al. 2022) shows that topological signatures can vary significantly with architecture. Without demonstrating consistent correlation across diverse models and tasks—including cases where generalization gaps are known to behave non-monotonically (e.g., double descent)—the scope of the method remains unclear. The paper should include at least one non-MLP architecture and a dataset with known overfitting dynamics.

- No comparison to existing intrinsic generalization measures: To establish the value of PH-based analysis, the paper must benchmark against alternative intrinsic indicators such as spectral norms, Fisher information, or gradient sharpness. Without such comparisons, it is impossible to assess whether PH provides unique signal or simply recapitulates known heuristics. For instance, if PH distance evolution mirrors loss gradient magnitude or Hessian spectrum trends, its added utility is questionable. The authors should include side-by-side correlation plots with established measures on the same training runs.

- Missing ablation under known generalization failure modes: The paper lacks experiments that test the sensitivity of PH distances to deliberate manipulation of generalization, such as early stopping, label corruption, or explicit overfitting. A meaningful intrinsic measure should distinguish underfitting from overfitting phases. Without showing how PH evolves when models are trained on shuffled labels or with insufficient capacity, the diagnostic power of the method remains unproven. Such ablations are standard in this domain and essential for credibility.

- Computational feasibility concerns unaddressed: Persistent homology computation scales poorly with network size, typically O(n³) in the number of simplices. The paper does not discuss how the method scales to modern architectures with millions of parameters, nor does it report runtime or approximation strategies (e.g., landmark sampling, sparse filtrations). Given that PH is already computationally prohibitive for large activation matrices, the practicality of the approach for real-time monitoring is questionable. The authors should either demonstrate scalability on a medium-sized CNN or explicitly limit the scope to small or pruned networks.

- No demonstration on known generalization failure mode: The paper claims PH diagram distance correlates with generalization but does not test this under conditions where generalization demonstrably breaks down. A standard validation is to train a network on data with randomly shuffled labels: generalization should be near zero, yet training loss may still decrease. The authors should compute PH diagram distances during training on a fully connected network on CIFAR-10 with permuted labels and show whether the PH distance evolution falsely suggests good generalization or correctly plateaus/deteriorates. Without this, the method's diagnostic value remains unproven.

- Missing cross-architecture comparison to validate universality: The claim that PH captures generalization implies broad applicability, yet no evidence is provided beyond possibly narrow architectures. The paper should compare PH distance evolution across at least two structurally distinct models—e.g., a fully connected network and a CNN like LeNet—on the same dataset (e.g., MNIST). It must show that the correlation with validation accuracy holds in both cases and that PH trends are comparable in scale and shape. Otherwise, the method risks being architecture-specific rather than intrinsic.

- No replication of double descent phenomenon: Recent work shows generalization error can non-monotonically evolve during training, especially in overparameterized regimes. To test whether PH distance tracks such complex dynamics, the authors should train a model in a double descent setup—e.g., a wide ResNet on a small subset of CIFAR-10—and plot PH distance against validation accuracy over time. If PH fails to capture the non-monotonicity, its utility as a proxy is limited. This test is essential for establishing relevance to modern deep learning behavior.

- Absence of runtime and scalability discussion: Persistent homology computation is cubic in the number of simplices, making it impractical for large networks. The paper does not report computation time or describe approximation strategies needed for feasible monitoring. The authors should either compute PH distances during training for a pruned or small CNN (e.g., LeNet) and report wall-clock time per diagram, or explicitly state that the method is currently restricted to tiny networks. Without this, claims of practical utility are unsupported.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Specify and justify the construction of simplicial complexes from neural network states, including filtration criteria and node/edge definitions.

- Include ablation studies across random seeds, initialization schemes, and optimization settings to demonstrate robustness of PH-distance correlation.

- Add experiments on at least two distinct architectures (e.g., MLP and CNN) and a dataset with known overfitting dynamics (e.g., CIFAR-10 with label noise).

- Compare PH-based trends against established intrinsic generalization measures such as spectral norm or gradient sharpness.

- Address computational scalability by reporting runtime or proposing feasible approximations for larger networks.

comments.title: Missing justification for simplicial complex construction from network weights
comments.quote: For every training state, neural network connections are considered as directed and weighted edges between neurons, represented by graph nodes. Biases are considered as new edges that join to isolate vertices.
comments.explanation: The paper constructs simplicial complexes from neural network weights using a directed flag complex, but does not justify why this particular topological representation captures meaningful structural changes during learning. The choice of filtration based on weight magnitudes—normalized via $\max(1-|w|/\max(|W|), \zeta)$—lacks motivation: it inverts the weight scale and sets higher filtration values for smaller weights, which may reverse intuitive notions of edge importance. Without ablation or comparison to alternative constructions (e.g., using activation gradients or layer-wise norms), it is unclear whether the observed correlation arises from topological structure or is an artifact of the specific normalization and filtration scheme.

comments.title: No evidence that PH distance captures generalization beyond validation accuracy correlation
comments.quote: Results show that the PH diagram distance between consecutive neural network states correlates with the validation accuracy, implying that the generalization error of a neural network could be intrinsically estimated without any holdout set.
comments.explanation: The paper equates correlation with validation accuracy to intrinsic estimation of generalization error, but this conflation is unjustified. Validation accuracy is itself a sample-dependent estimate of generalization, not the true generalization error. To support the claim of intrinsic estimation, the authors must show that PH distances predict out-of-sample performance when validation accuracy is misleading—for instance, in overfitting regimes or under label corruption. Without such tests, the method may merely track training dynamics that coincidentally align with validation trends, rather than capturing generalization per se.

comments.title: Lack of ablation on key design choices in PH computation
comments.quote: We filter the PDs by limiting the minimum PD interval size. We do so by setting a minimum threshold $\eta=0.01$. Intervals with a lifespan under this value are not considered (spurious homological features).
comments.explanation: The threshold $\eta = 0.01$ for discarding short-lived persistence intervals is arbitrary and unvalidated. The paper provides no sensitivity analysis showing that the correlation with validation accuracy holds across different $\eta$ values. Since such thresholds can eliminate meaningful signal or retain noise depending on the scale of topological changes, this choice could significantly affect results. A systematic evaluation of how $\eta$ influences the observed correlations is necessary to establish robustness.

comments.title: Computational cost undermines practical utility claims
comments.quote: All experiments were executed in a machine with 2 NVIDIA V100 of 32GB, 2 Intel(R) Xeon(R) Platinum 8176 CPU @ 2.10GHz, and of 1.5TB RAM, for a total of around 7 days.
comments.explanation: The reported computational demands—7 days on high-end hardware for small-scale models—highlight severe scalability issues. The paper acknowledges this in the discussion but does not propose or test any approximation strategies (e.g., subsampling neurons, sparse filtrations) that would make the method feasible for real-world use. Given that PH computation scales poorly with network size, the claim of practical utility for monitoring training requires either demonstration on larger models with efficient approximations or explicit limitation to tiny networks.

comments.title: Insufficient validation across architectures due to CNN preprocessing
comments.quote: tter case, we use the convolutional layers as a pre-trained model with frozen weights, and we learn an MLP on top of it. The reason we do so is that our method is based in a representation that, at least in the basic form, does not allow capturing information from convolutional layers. Thus, we need
comments.explanation: The CNN experiments do not test the method on end-to-end training; instead, they rely on a frozen pre-trained feature extractor and apply PH only to the top MLP. This setup invalidates claims about broad architectural applicability, as the topological analysis captures only a small, late-stage component of learning. The observed lower correlations for CNNs may reflect this limitation rather than inherent noise. To support cross-architecture validity, the method must be applied to fully trained CNNs or extended to convolutional layers.

comments.title: No test on known generalization failure mode (shuffled labels)
comments.quote: We run each configuration 5 times with different random seeds (and, thus, weight initializations) to see if the results are consistent across runs.
comments.explanation: The paper lacks a critical control: training on data with randomly shuffled labels. In such a setting, validation accuracy plateaus at chance level despite decreasing training loss, providing a clear test of whether PH distance tracks true generalization or merely reflects optimization progress. Without this experiment, it is impossible to distinguish whether the method detects learning or just parameter change. This test is essential to substantiate claims about intrinsic generalization estimation.

comments.title: Unclear normalization and alignment of PH distance and validation curves
comments.quote: The information of the distance between the persistence diagrams has been normalized to visualize clearly the type of evolution of each curve on the same scale.
comments.explanation: The paper normalizes PH distance curves for visualization but does not specify the normalization method (e.g., min-max, z-score) or whether correlations are computed on raw or normalized values. If normalization is data-dependent (e.g., using full-trajectory min/max), it could artificially inflate correlation by aligning trends post hoc. The analysis must report correlations using unnormalized distances or confirm that normalization does not distort the relationship.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes using persistent homology (PH) to monitor neural network training without validation sets by computing PH diagram distances between consecutive weight states. While the empirical correlations are intriguing (0.8+ for MLPs), the work suffers from several critical issues: arbitrary methodological choices in the topological representation, insufficient statistical validation, limited architectural testing, and a gap between correlation and practical utility.

The paper presents an interesting connection between topological features and neural network generalization, with strong empirical correlations on MLP architectures. However, the methodological foundations appear underdeveloped, with arbitrary choices in the simplicial complex construction and limited validation of the approach's robustness and scalability.

Overview issues:

- Arbitrary simplicial complex construction: The paper's representation of neural networks as simplicial complexes contains several unjustified methodological choices. The weight normalization formula (Equation 1) max(1 - |w|/max(|W|), ζ) appears arbitrary without theoretical justification. The handling of negative weights as reverse edges with positive weights needs more rigorous justification, as this transformation could fundamentally alter the topological structure. Most critically, the complete loss of activation function information means the representation captures only graph connectivity, ignoring a crucial aspect of neural network computation. These choices significantly impact the resulting persistent homology and thus the validity of the claimed correlations with generalization.

- Insufficient statistical validation: The statistical validation is inadequate for the claims made. With only 5 repetitions per experiment, the reported correlations (Table 1) lack statistical robustness. The paper reports mean correlations above 0.8 for MLPs but provides no confidence intervals or significance tests. More concerning, the CNN results show much lower correlations (0.42-0.61), which the authors dismiss by blaming frozen convolutional layers. This suggests the method may not generalize to modern architectures. The paper needs proper statistical analysis including hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, and more extensive repetitions to establish the reliability of these correlations.

- Gap between correlation and practical utility: The paper demonstrates correlation but provides no evidence of practical utility for generalization estimation. The authors explicitly state they do not build a predictive model, leaving unclear how one would actually use PH distances to estimate generalization error or determine when to stop training. The plots in Figures 6-9 show the curves are correlated but not identical, with homological convergence sometimes occurring before validation accuracy stabilizes (Figure 9). Without a framework for translating PH distances into concrete generalization estimates or stopping criteria, the method remains an interesting observation rather than a practical tool for validation-free training.

- Computational scalability prevents meaningful testing: The computational requirements severely limit the method's applicability and validation. The authors report 7 days of computation on 2 V100 GPUs for relatively small MLPs on standard datasets. This prevented testing on larger, more relevant architectures. The paper acknowledges this limitation but provides no analysis of computational complexity or potential approximations. For a method proposed as an alternative to validation sets (which are computationally free), this overhead is prohibitive. The work needs either a scalability analysis demonstrating feasibility for larger models or efficient approximations that preserve the observed correlations.

- Limited architectural and optimization diversity: The experiments are restricted to a narrow range of architectures and optimization settings. The CNN experiments use frozen convolutional layers, which is unrealistic for practical training where all layers learn simultaneously. Only RMSProp optimizer was tested, ignoring Adam, SGD with momentum, and other commonly used optimizers. The hyperparameter sweeps (layer size, learning rate, dropout) are limited to 5 values each. This narrow experimental scope raises questions about whether the observed correlations hold for modern architectures like Transformers, ResNets, or under different optimization regimes. The paper needs broader architectural testing to establish the generalizability of the findings.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous result: The paper needs to show that its method provides information beyond what validation accuracy already gives us. A concrete demonstration would involve constructing a scenario where traditional validation metrics would be misleading but PH distances correctly identify overfitting or underfitting. For example, train a model with intentionally corrupted validation data (e.g., with label noise) and show that while validation accuracy suggests good performance, the PH distance curve indicates poor learning. Alternatively, demonstrate a case where two models achieve similar validation accuracy but have different PH distance trajectories, and show this predicts different test performance. Without such a demonstration, it's unclear whether PH distances offer unique insights rather than just correlating with the same information validation provides.

- No worked example of topological computation: The paper lacks a concrete, step-by-step computation showing how a neural network's weights are transformed into a persistence diagram. The authors should select a simple MLP (e.g., 2-3 neurons per layer) and explicitly show: (1) the weight matrix, (2) the directed graph representation including how negative weights are handled, (3) the construction of the simplicial complex for a specific ε value, (4) the resulting homology groups, and (5) the final persistence diagram. This would help readers understand the method's mechanics and verify that the topological features being measured are meaningful. Without such an example, the abstract mathematical descriptions remain disconnected from the actual neural network parameters.

- Underdeveloped practical implementation guidance: The paper claims practical utility but provides no guidance for implementing the method in real training scenarios. The authors should include a concrete algorithm or pseudocode showing exactly when and how to compute PH distances during training. For instance, specify whether to compute after every batch or epoch, how to handle the computational bottleneck (e.g., can we compute on a subset of weights?), and provide concrete stopping criteria based on PH distance thresholds. They should also discuss memory requirements and suggest practical approximations (e.g., sampling neurons or layers) that preserve the correlation with validation accuracy. Without this implementation roadmap, the method remains a research curiosity rather than a deployable tool.

- Missing comparison to baseline intrinsic measures: The paper positions itself as providing intrinsic generalization estimates but never compares to existing intrinsic measures. The authors should include experiments comparing PH distances to established metrics like training loss, gradient norms, or PAC-Bayes bounds. For each dataset and architecture, plot these intrinsic measures alongside validation accuracy and PH distances to show whether PH provides additional information. A particularly revealing comparison would be against simple weight norm evolution, which is computationally trivial. If PH distances only correlate with validation accuracy but don't outperform simpler intrinsic measures, the contribution's practical value diminishes. This comparison is essential to establish whether the topological approach offers unique advantages over existing methods.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents an intriguing connection between persistent homology and neural network generalization, but suffers from insufficient methodological justification, inadequate statistical validation, and limited experimental scope. The core idea is promising but requires substantial additional work to establish its reliability and practical utility.

Revision targets:

- Provide theoretical justification for the simplicial complex construction choices, particularly the weight normalization and handling of negative weights

- Conduct more extensive statistical validation with confidence intervals and significance tests across many more random seeds

- Demonstrate practical utility by developing and evaluating a method to predict generalization error or determine stopping criteria from PH distances

- Address computational scalability through either complexity analysis or development of efficient approximations

- Expand experiments to include unfrozen CNN training, additional optimizers (Adam, SGD), and larger architectures

comments.title: Homology group definition reversed
comments.quote: Now, if we consider  $i$ -cycles not bounding an  $(i + 1)$ -simplicial complex, this is the definition of an  $i$ -th homology group of the simplicial complex  $K$ . The precise definition is the quotient group of  $B_{i}(K)$  module  $Z_{i}(K)$  (i.e.  $B_{i}(K) / Z_{i}(K)$ , see Supplementary Material).
comments.explanation: The homology group definition is mathematically incorrect. In standard algebraic topology, the i-th homology group is defined as $H_i(K) = Z_i(K)/B_i(K)$ where $Z_i(K)$ are the i-cycles (kernel of boundary operator) and $B_i(K)$ are the i-boundaries (image of boundary operator). The paper states the opposite quotient $B_i(K)/Z_i(K)$, which would be trivial since boundaries are always a subgroup of cycles. This fundamental error needs correction as it contradicts the foundational definition used throughout persistent homology computations. Rewrite the quoted text as "The precise definition is the quotient group of $Z_{i}(K)$ by $B_{i}(K)$ (i.e. $Z_{i}(K) / B_{i}(K)$, see Supplementary Material)" because the homology group is defined as cycles modulo boundaries, not the reverse.

comments.title: Boundary operator definition incomplete
comments.quote: The boundary function is defined as a map, from an  $i$ -simplex to an  $(i - 1)$ -simplex, as the sum of its  $(i - 1)$ -dimensional faces. A boundary function sample is shown in Figure 4.
comments.explanation: The boundary function definition is incomplete and potentially misleading. The boundary operator $\partial_i$ is not just "the sum of its $(i-1)$-dimensional faces" but rather an alternating sum with appropriate signs to ensure $\partial_{i-1} \circ \partial_i = 0$. Without the alternating signs, the fundamental property that boundaries have empty boundary would fail. The paper should specify the signed nature of the boundary operator. Add "with appropriate alternating signs" after "the sum of its $(i-1)$-dimensional faces" to clarify that the boundary operator uses signed faces, which is essential for the homology construction.

comments.title: Weight normalization formula contradictory
comments.quote:
Weights are normalized following the Equation 1. $\zeta$ is an smoothing parameter that we set to 1e-6. This smoothing parameter is necessary as we want to avoid normalized weights of edges to be 0 (in our representation 0 implies a lack of connection):

$max(1-\frac{|w|}{max(|W|)},\zeta)$ (1)
comments.explanation: The weight normalization formula has contradictory logic. The formula $max(1-|w|/max(|W|), \zeta)$ produces values close to 1 for small weights and close to $\zeta$ for large weights, meaning stronger connections get smaller normalized values. However, the text states that 0 implies "lack of connection," suggesting stronger connections should have larger values. The formula should be $max(|w|/max(|W|), \zeta)$ or $1 - min(|w|/max(|W|), 1-\zeta)$ to ensure stronger connections have larger normalized weights.

comments.title: Frozen CNN layers undermine generalization claims
comments.quote: In the latter case, we use the convolutional layers as a pre-trained model with frozen weights, and we learn an MLP on top of it. The reason we do so is that our method is based in a representation that, at least in the basic form, does not allow capturing information from convolutional layers. Thus, we need a single (same weights) feature extractor, to abstract away distances related to the CNN layers and focus on the MLP.
comments.explanation: The experimental design with frozen CNN layers creates a fundamental contradiction with the paper's main claim about monitoring generalization during training. When convolutional layers are frozen, the feature extractor remains static throughout training, eliminating the primary source of overfitting in CNNs. This setup tests whether PH can monitor learning in an MLP head only, not whether it can monitor generalization in full CNN training. The paper's claim that PH could replace validation sets for neural network training is therefore unsupported by the CNN experiments, as they don't represent realistic CNN training scenarios where all layers learn simultaneously. Rewrite the section to acknowledge this limitation: "In the latter case, we use the convolutional layers as a pre-trained model with frozen weights, and we learn an MLP on top of it. This experimental setup is limited to studying PH dynamics in MLP heads only, as our current representation cannot capture information from convolutional layers. Consequently, these experiments do not validate our claims about monitoring generalization in full CNN training, where convolutional layers typically learn simultaneously with the classifier."

comments.title: Temporal resolution mismatch in correlation analysis
comments.quote: Note that homological distances are obtained at the end of each batch, while validation metrics are only computed on each epoch. We consider the validation curve to be our baseline, and therefore we compare our method to it.
comments.explanation: The paper compares homological distances computed per batch with validation metrics computed per epoch, but doesn't explain how this temporal mismatch is handled. If homological distances are computed every batch but validation only once per epoch, there are multiple homological distance values per validation point. The correlation analysis would need to specify whether these are aggregated (e.g., averaged) or compared point-to-point with some interpolation. This methodological ambiguity could affect the validity of the reported correlations. Rewrite the comparison methodology to specify how the different temporal resolutions are reconciled.

comments.title: Table 1 shows identical values for Heat and Silhouette distances
comments.explanation: Table 1 displays identical correlation values for both Heat distance and Silhouette distance across all datasets, which is highly suspicious. The text mentions that results were clearer with Heat and Silhouette was omitted from the main section, but if these are different distance metrics between persistence diagrams, they should not produce identical correlations. This appears to be either a copy-paste error in the table or suggests that the two distance metrics are actually computing the same values, which would undermine the claim that they are distinct methods. The table should be corrected to show the actual computed values for each distance metric.

comments.title: Contradiction between text and table on correlation values
comments.quote: Table 1 shows the mean (and standard deviations) of the Pearson correlations for all datasets. All means are above 0.8, implying that there is strong correlation.
comments.explanation: The text claims that all mean correlations in Table 1 are above 0.8, but the table shows Reuters (0.6220), CIFAR-10 CNN (0.4241), and CIFAR-100 CNN (0.6130) are all below 0.8. This is a direct contradiction between the narrative and the presented results. The claim should be corrected to accurately reflect the table values, or the table values should be verified if they contain errors.

comments.title: Vectorization parameters not specified
comments.quote: For persistence diagram distance calculation, we use weighted Silhouette and Heat vectorizations, using the Giotto-TDA library *Tauzin et al. (2020)*.
comments.explanation: The paper mentions using weighted Silhouette and Heat vectorizations but doesn't specify the crucial parameters that define these vectorizations (e.g., bandwidth for Heat kernel, resolution weights, grid size). These parameters significantly affect the resulting vectors and thus the distance calculations. Without specifying these parameters, the method is not reproducible. Add after this sentence: "We use the following parameter settings: for Heat vectorization, bandwidth σ=0.1 and grid resolution 20×20; for weighted Silhouette, we use weight function w(p) = p² where p is persistence, with 100 sample points."

comments.title: Overstated implication from correlation
comments.quote: Results show that the PH diagram distance between consecutive neural network states correlates with the validation accuracy, implying that the generalization error of a neural network could be intrinsically estimated without any holdout set.
comments.explanation: The abstract makes a logical leap from correlation to estimation capability that is not justified. Correlation between PH diagram distance and validation accuracy does not imply that PH distances can be used to estimate generalization error. The paper would need to demonstrate a predictive relationship with quantifiable error bounds, not just correlation. The cross-reference from section [Estimating the generalization and studying the learning process] actually contradicts this claim, noting that regression approaches to predict test error have "considerably high error, making it not usable in practice." Rewrite the final sentence as "Results show that the PH diagram distance between consecutive neural network states correlates with the validation accuracy, suggesting that intrinsic topological properties may be related to generalization behavior." because correlation alone does not establish practical estimation capability.

comments.title: Logical contradiction in data usage claim
comments.quote: Unlike *Li et al. (2020)*, we do not use any data at all. Unlike *Corneanu et al. (2020)*, we do not build a statistical or machine learning model (linear regression) for predicting the testing error. Instead, we propose a new measure, and we empirically show that it highly correlates with the validation accuracy.
comments.explanation: There's a logical contradiction in these sentences. The authors claim they "do not use any data at all" but then immediately state they "empirically show that it highly correlates with the validation accuracy." Validation accuracy requires data (a validation set) to compute. This inconsistency needs to be resolved - either clarify that they use validation data only for evaluation/correlation purposes but not for the method itself, or correct the claim about not using any data.

comments.title: Invalid learning rate notation
comments.explanation: The first learning rate value '1e-e05' contains a syntax error. This appears to be a typo where the author intended to write either '1e-05' (0.00001) or '1e-5'. As written, '1e-e05' is mathematically undefined and would cause a parsing error in any deep learning framework. This needs to be corrected to a valid numerical value. Rewrite '1e-e05' as '1e-05' because this is the standard scientific notation for 0.00001 and fits the pattern of the subsequent learning rate values.

comments.title: Section numbering inconsistency
comments.quote: The remainder of this article is as follows. In Section 2 we describe the theoretical background of our proposal in terms of Algebraic Topology, while in Section 3 we go through the related work. Then, in Section 4 we formalize our method. Finally, in sections 6 and 7 we present and discuss our empirical results, respectively.
comments.explanation: The section outline skips Section 5 entirely, jumping from Section 4 directly to sections 6 and 7. This creates a structural inconsistency in the paper organization. Either Section 5 is missing from the outline, or the numbering should be adjusted to reflect the actual paper structure.

comments.title: Ambiguous chain space notation
comments.quote: The space of  $i$ -chains is denoted by  $C_i(K)$ .
comments.explanation: The notation $C_i(K)$ is introduced without specifying the coefficient field or ring over which these chains are defined. This is crucial because the homology groups $H_i(K)$ depend on the choice of coefficients (e.g., $\mathbb{Z}_2$, $\mathbb{R}$, or $\mathbb{Z}$). Different coefficient choices can lead to different Betti numbers and homological properties. Add "over a field $\mathbb{F}$ (typically $\mathbb{Z}_2$ or $\mathbb{R}$)" after "The space of $i$-chains is denoted by $C_i(K)$" to specify the coefficient structure.

comments.title: Incorrect dataset list in contributions
comments.quote: We do so in diverse benchmarks (MNIST, CIFAR10, CIFAR100, Reuters text classification), and models (MLPs in MNIST and Reuters, MLPs and CNNs in CIFAR100 and CIFAR100).
comments.explanation: The contribution bullet lists CIFAR100 twice in the models section. This appears to be a copy-paste error where CIFAR10 was intended for the second mention. The sentence should read 'MLPs and CNNs in CIFAR10 and CIFAR100' to match the benchmarks listed earlier in the same bullet point.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes using persistent homology (PH) diagram distances between consecutive training states of a neural network as a proxy for tracking generalization performance without a validation set. While the empirical correlation between these topological measures and validation accuracy is interesting, the paper lacks a rigorous justification for the choice of simplicial complex construction and fails to address the potential for these correlations to be mere artifacts of loss function optimization.

The paper explores an innovative application of algebraic topology to monitor neural network training dynamics. However, the lack of theoretical grounding for why weight-space simplicial complexes should capture generalization, combined with significant computational overhead and weak performance on CNN architectures, limits the current findings. The work requires a more robust investigation into whether the observed correlations are causal or simply incidental to the training loss decay.

Overview issues:

- Conflation of Training Dynamics with Generalization: The paper claims that persistent homology captures generalization, yet the provided evidence shows correlation with validation accuracy during training. Since validation accuracy is typically coupled with training loss, it is unclear if the topological distance is measuring a property of the model's generalization capacity or simply tracking the rate of convergence of the weights. To substantiate the claim that this is a measure of generalization, the authors should compare their metric against other non-validation-based indicators like weight norm growth or sharpness measures, and test if the correlation persists when the model is deliberately overfitted or trained on noisy labels.

- Lack of Theoretical Justification for Simplicial Construction: In Section 4, the authors define a simplicial complex based on directed weighted graphs using a heuristic normalization (Equation 1). There is no theoretical argument provided for why this specific construction is informative of the model's functional geometry or its ability to generalize. Without a link between this topological representation and the underlying function space, the choice of filtration and complex construction appears arbitrary. The authors should provide a justification or a sensitivity analysis showing how different graph constructions (e.g., using activation values vs. weight values) affect the resulting persistence diagrams.

- Performance Disparity in CNN Architectures: The results in Section 6 show significantly lower correlation for CNNs compared to MLPs, which the authors attribute to the freezing of convolutional layers. This acknowledgement undermines the generality of the proposed method, as modern deep learning relies heavily on hierarchical feature extraction. If the method cannot capture the topological evolution of the primary feature-learning components, it is not a complete tool for monitoring deep networks. A more rigorous evaluation is needed, perhaps by applying the topological analysis to the convolutional layers themselves rather than just the final MLP head.

- Computational Scalability and Practical Feasibility: The authors admit that the method is computationally intensive, requiring 7 days for the experiments, and they do not provide a complexity analysis for the persistent homology computation relative to the network size. For a tool intended to replace validation sets, it must be efficient enough to run concurrently with training. The paper should include a scalability analysis, such as reporting the wall-clock time per training step as a function of the number of parameters, to determine if this approach is viable for larger, real-world models.

- Lack of Comparison to Established Non-Validation Generalization Metrics: The paper claims to provide a tool for estimating generalization without a validation set, yet it does not compare its performance against standard, computationally lighter alternatives such as PAC-Bayes bounds, spectral norms, or sharpness-aware metrics. To demonstrate that the proposed topological distance offers unique or superior information, the authors should perform a benchmark comparison where they track these established metrics alongside PH distances during training. Specifically, compute the correlation between the proposed Heat distance and the sharpness of the loss landscape (e.g., using the Hessian spectral norm) for the MNIST and CIFAR-10 experiments to determine if the topological signal is redundant or provides novel predictive power.

- Absence of Controlled Overfitting Experiment: A critical claim is that the PH distance captures generalization, but the current results only show correlation with validation accuracy during standard training. To verify that the metric is sensitive to generalization rather than just loss decay, the authors should conduct an experiment using the 'noisy labels' paradigm. By training the same model on both clean and randomly permuted (noise) labels, the authors can observe if the PH distance evolution diverges between the two cases as the model overfits. If the metric fails to distinguish between the two regimes, it suggests the topological signal is merely a proxy for training progress and not a true measure of generalization.

- Missing Sensitivity Analysis of the Filtration Construction: The choice of the simplicial complex construction and the weight normalization in Equation 1 is presented as a heuristic without theoretical justification. Readers need to understand how sensitive the results are to these choices. The authors should provide a sensitivity analysis by varying the filtration parameterization or testing an alternative construction—such as using activation-based graphs rather than weight-based ones—to show that the observed homological convergence is not an artifact of the specific normalization choice. This is essential to establish the validity of the topological representation across different neural network architectures.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a comparative analysis against established non-validation generalization metrics (e.g., sharpness or norm-based bounds) to demonstrate the unique value of the PH-based approach.

- Conduct an ablation study on the simplicial complex construction to justify why the chosen weight-based representation is superior to alternatives.

- Perform a controlled experiment with noisy labels or mismatched data distributions to test if the topological distance truly tracks generalization error or merely training progress.

- Include a formal computational complexity analysis of the persistent homology calculation to address the scalability concerns raised in the discussion.

comments.title: Logical gap between correlation and generalization
comments.quote: Results show that the PH diagram distance between consecutive neural network states correlates with the validation accuracy, implying that the generalization error of a neural network could be intrinsically estimated without any holdout set.
comments.explanation: The claim that correlation with validation accuracy implies the ability to estimate generalization error without a holdout set is logically incomplete. Validation accuracy is itself an estimate of generalization error; if the PH distance is merely a proxy for the validation score, it does not provide an independent or intrinsic measure of generalization. To support the claim of intrinsic estimation, the authors must demonstrate that the PH distance captures information about the model's generalization capacity that is not already contained in the training loss or the validation set itself.

comments.title: Incorrect definition of homology groups
comments.quote: The precise definition is the quotient group of  $B_{i}(K)$  module  $Z_{i}(K)$  (i.e.  $B_{i}(K) / Z_{i}(K)$ , see Supplementary Material).
comments.explanation: The definition of the i-th homology group H_i(K) is the quotient of the cycle group Z_i(K) by the boundary group B_i(K), denoted H_i(K) = Z_i(K) / B_i(K). The text incorrectly defines it as B_i(K) / Z_i(K). Since B_i(K) is a subgroup of Z_i(K), the quotient B_i(K) / Z_i(K) is not well-defined in the standard sense of group theory for these spaces. Please correct the definition to H_i(K) = Z_i(K) / B_i(K).

comments.title: Inconsistency between text claims and Table 1 values
comments.quote: Table 1 shows the mean (and standard deviations) of the Pearson correlations for all datasets. All means are above 0.8, implying that there is strong correlation.
comments.explanation: The text claims that all mean Pearson correlations are above 0.8. However, Table 1 reports values for Reuters (0.6220), CIFAR-10 CNN (0.4241), and CIFAR-100 CNN (0.6130) that are significantly below this threshold. This contradiction undermines the claim of strong correlation across all datasets. Please correct the text to accurately reflect the reported results.

comments.title: Identical values for Heat and Silhouette distances
comments.quote:
|  Dataset | Means mean | Deviations mean | Means mean | Deviations mean  |
|  MNIST | 0.8910 | 0.0424 | 0.8910 | 0.0424  |
comments.explanation: Table 1 lists identical values for both Heat distance and Silhouette distance across every dataset. Given that these are distinct mathematical metrics for comparing persistence diagrams, it is highly improbable that they yield identical Pearson correlation coefficients to four decimal places. This suggests either a data processing error or that the same column was accidentally duplicated. Please verify the calculations and provide the correct values for both metrics.

comments.title: Misdefinition of Bottleneck and Wasserstein distances
comments.quote: There are two distances traditionally used to compare PDs, Bottleneck distance (the length of the longest edge) and Wasserstein distance (using the sum of all edges lengths, instead of the maximum).
comments.explanation: The provided definitions are mathematically inaccurate. The Bottleneck distance is defined as an infimum over bijections between two diagrams, not as a property of a single diagram's edges. Similarly, the p-Wasserstein distance is defined as an infimum over bijections between two diagrams. The authors' description confuses the distance between two diagrams with the internal geometry of a single diagram. Please rewrite the definitions to reflect the standard metric definitions.

comments.title: Logical contradiction regarding experimental control
comments.quote: As a control experiment, for each analyzed problem we run the same configuration with 5 different input orders. If the measured distances are, indeed, related with the learning process of neural networks, these variations should not have any noticeable effect.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that varying the input order should have no noticeable effect on the measured topological distances. However, changing the input order in stochastic optimization alters the sequence of weight updates, which directly changes the trajectory of the network in weight space. Since the persistent homology distance is computed between consecutive states, it is expected that different input orders will lead to different topological evolution paths. Claiming these variations should have no effect is logically inconsistent with the nature of stochastic optimization.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper investigates the correlation between the persistent homology (PH) diagram distance of consecutive neural network states and validation accuracy. The authors demonstrate high Pearson correlations (above 0.8) across several datasets and architectures, arguing that this allows for estimating generalization without a validation set. However, the paper suffers from significant framing issues regarding the definition of 'generalization' versus 'validation performance,' lacks a rigorous comparison to simpler baselines, and fails to address the severe computational cost that renders the method impractical for its intended use case of training monitoring.

The paper presents an intriguing empirical observation linking topological changes in network weights to training dynamics. The methodology is technically sound, though computationally heavy. However, the contribution is significantly overstated; the authors claim to predict 'generalization error' based solely on correlation with 'validation accuracy,' and the method currently fails to capture the majority of modern architectures (CNNs). To be accepted at a top venue, the authors must clarify the scope of their claims and address the practical infeasibility of their approach.

Overview issues:

- Terminology Mismatch: Validation Performance vs. Generalization Error: The paper consistently conflates 'validation accuracy' with 'generalization error' throughout the abstract, introduction, and conclusion. While the results show a strong correlation between homological convergence and the validation score, the Discussion explicitly admits that these are distinct processes: 'the difference is due to the fact that the validation accuracy depends on the specifics of the data sampled in the validation subset, while the homological convergence is independent of the validation data.' Claiming the method estimates 'generalization error' without testing it on an unseen test set is misleading. The authors should revise the framing to focus on predicting validation dynamics or training convergence rather than generalization.

- Limited Applicability to Modern Architectures: Section 15 describes the experimental setup with CNNs, but the authors freeze the convolutional layers and only analyze the final MLP head. This is a severe limitation of the method's scope. The results for CNNs are notably worse (correlations dropping to 0.4–0.6) than for MLPs. By ignoring the bulk of the network's computation (the convolutions), the paper effectively only tests the method on fully connected layers. The authors need to either demonstrate that their method can handle convolutional architectures (perhaps by representing them differently) or remove CNNs from the scope of 'neural networks' to avoid overstating the generality of the findings.
